name: Extra-Easy Apple Streusel
recipeIngredient: 3 tbsp Diet margarine, plus 1 tsp divided
6 tbsp Granulated sugar substitute, divided (see note)
recipeInstructions:
NOTE: Use a sugar substitute that can be used spoon-for-spoon in place of sugar, such as Sprinkle Sweet or Sugar Twin. Do not use concentrated artificial sweeteners like those found in single-serving packets.
1. Spray a 9-inch glass pie pan with cooking spray.
2. Combine biscuit mix with the 3 tablespoons diet margarine in a blender or food processor. Process until blended to fine crumbs (mixture will not form a batter; it will have the consistency of heavy flour).
3. Sprinkle half the flour mixture on the bottom of the pie pan.
4. Spoon half the apples on top of the flour mixture.
5. Sprinkle 2 tablespoons of the sugar substitute on top.
6. Spoon the remaining apples, including any juice in the can, on top.
7. If desired, sprinkle the grated peel over the apples.
8. Add the cinnamon and the remaining 1 teaspoon diet margarine to the remaining flour mixture. Process into crumbs.
9. Sprinkle the crumbs evenly over the apples.
10. Bake at 400°F for 20 minutes, just until crumbs are lightly browned (do not overbake or sugar substitute will lose its sweetness).
11. Remove from oven and allow to cool completely.
12. Stir remaining 2 tablespoons of granulated sugar substitute with confectioners' sugar, if desired, until blended. Sprinkle over the top of the cooled pie. Alternatively, omit confectioners' sugar and sprinkle the top of the cooled pie with sugar-free sweetener.
(120 calories per serving; 10 additional with confectioners' sugar)
WITH FRUCTOSE:
If you prefer, granulated fructose (also known as fruit sugar) may be used instead of sugar-free sweetener. Substitute 1 teaspoon lemon juice for the lemon peel.
WITH OTHER CANNED FRUITS:
Three cups canned unsweetened sliced peaches, diced pears, pineapple tidbits, fruit cocktail, or other juice-packed fruits may be substituted for the apples. If other fruits are used, drain them and reserve 1/2 cup of the juice to pour over the fruit before adding the final topping of crumbs.
